1:
"And afterward Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness." The witnesses note the first demand to Pharaoh — "Let my people go" — the crux of the deliverance summons. On "Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh," the grammatical voices note the approach. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel" — Moses and Aaron, having gathered the elders (Exodus 4:29-31), went in to Pharaoh with God's message; the first demand; the bold approach (the two brothers before the great king); the embassy of the LORD.

On "Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go," interpreters expound the demand. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great word — "Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness": Moses delivered God's command, "Let my people go" — the demand that runs through the whole contest (Exodus 7:16; 8:1, 20; 9:1; 10:3), God claiming Israel as "my people" (his by covenant, not Pharaoh's slaves), and requiring their release to serve (worship) him; the authority of the demand ("Thus saith the LORD," the prophetic embassy, the word of the Sovereign to the proud king); the purpose (to hold a feast, to worship and sacrifice, the service of God the end of the deliverance — redeemed to serve, Exodus 4:23; the people freed that they might worship); the keynote of the Exodus.

On "that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the worship: "that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness" — the immediate request (a three days' journey to sacrifice, v.3); the worship of God the purpose; the deliverance unto service (the redeemed people called to serve their God, not Pharaoh); the demand that began the contest. The verse displays the first demand to Pharaoh.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses and Aaron told Pharaoh, "Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go" (Keil, Calvin, Henry, Wesley). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the deliverance summons: "Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me" — God claiming Israel as his own people (his by covenant), demanding their release to worship him; the authority of "Thus saith the LORD" (the prophetic embassy to the proud king); the deliverance unto service (redeemed to serve God, Exodus 4:23); the keynote of the whole Exodus contest. The verse displays the deliverance-summons crux. The verse marks the demand: Let my people go. The verse displays the LORD: who claims them. The verse displays the purpose: to worship. The verse displays the embassy: prophetic. The demand displays God claiming his people from Pharaoh. The verse displays the Moses: demanding. The verse displays the Pharaoh: summoned. The verse displays the contest: begun. The first demand to Pharaoh displays the crux: thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me (Exodus 7:16; 4:23) — God claiming Israel as his own, demanding their release to serve him, the keynote of the Exodus. The verse displays the defiant Pharaoh (v.2).
2:
"And Pharaoh said, Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice to let Israel go? I know not the LORD, neither will I let Israel go." The witnesses note Pharaoh's proud defiance of the LORD — the crux of the denial of God. On "Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice," interpreters expound the defiance. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Pharaoh's words — "Who is the LORD (Jehovah), that I should obey his voice to let Israel go?": Pharaoh contemptuously demanded who this "LORD" (Jehovah, the God of the Hebrews) was, that he, the great king of Egypt, should obey him; the proud defiance (the haughty contempt of God by the worldly potentate); the challenge to God's authority (Pharaoh, himself accounted a god, refusing to acknowledge the LORD); the arrogance that set the proud king against the Almighty; the contest joined (the question "Who is the LORD?" that the plagues would answer, Pharaoh learning, to his ruin, who the LORD is, Exodus 7:5, 17; 9:16; 14:18, "the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD").

On "I know not the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the ignorance. Clarke and Ellicott note "I know not the LORD" — Pharaoh's ignorance (and willful refusal to acknowledge Jehovah); not that he was ignorant of the Hebrews' God by report, but he "knew not" in the sense of refusing to own his authority (as he "knew not Joseph," Exodus 1:8); the practical atheism of the proud (acknowledging not God, Psalm 14:1; the heart that says, "who is the LORD?", Proverbs 30:9); the defiant unbelief.

On "neither will I let Israel go," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the refusal: "neither will I let Israel go" — Pharaoh's flat refusal (the hardened will against God); the defiance that would be broken by the plagues; the providence (God's purpose to show his power on the proud king, Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17, "for this same purpose have I raised thee up, that I might shew my power in thee"); the contest between the proud king and the Almighty begun. The verse displays Pharaoh's proud defiance of the LORD.

The witnesses agree the substance: Pharaoh proudly demanded "Who is the LORD?", refused to know him, and would not let Israel go (Calvin, Clarke, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the denial of God: "Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice?... I know not the LORD" — the proud potentate's contemptuous defiance of God's authority, the practical atheism that acknowledges not God (Psalm 14:1), the willful refusal (as "knew not Joseph," Exodus 1:8); the question the plagues would answer (Pharaoh learning who the LORD is, Exodus 7:5; 14:18), the contest of the proud king against the Almighty, raised up that God might show his power (Romans 9:17). The verse displays the denial-of-God crux. The verse marks the defiance: who is the LORD. The verse displays the ignorance: I know not the LORD. The verse displays the refusal: will not let Israel go. The verse displays the contest: joined. The defiance displays the proud king against God. The verse displays the Pharaoh: defying. The verse displays the LORD: denied. The verse displays the plagues: to answer. Pharaoh's proud defiance of the LORD displays the crux: who is the LORD? I know not the LORD, neither will I let Israel go (Psalm 14:1; Exodus 1:8; 7:5; Romans 9:17) — the proud denial of God's authority, the question the plagues would answer, the contest of the king against the Almighty. The verse displays the pleading Moses (v.3).
3:
"And they said, The God of the Hebrews hath met with us: let us go, we pray thee, three days' journey into the desert, and sacrifice unto the LORD our God; lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword." The witnesses note Moses and Aaron pressing the request to sacrifice. On "The God of the Hebrews hath met with us," the grammatical voices note the message. Ellicott and Gill note that "they said, The God of the Hebrews hath met with us: let us go... three days' journey into the desert, and sacrifice unto the LORD our God" — Moses and Aaron, meeting Pharaoh's defiance (v.2), pressed the request: the God of the Hebrews had met with them (at the bush, Exodus 3), and required them to go three days' journey to sacrifice; the renewed appeal; the modest request (a journey to worship, the immediate demand, behind which lay the full deliverance).

On "let us go... three days' journey into the desert," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the request. Calvin and Henry note the request (a three days' journey to sacrifice, away from Egypt where their worship would offend the Egyptians, Exodus 8:26) — the immediate demand; the test of Pharaoh (whether he would grant even this); the worship of God the purpose.

On "lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the warning: "lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword" — the warning (that God might punish them if they failed to worship); the reverent fear of God (and, the witnesses note, a hint to Pharaoh of the judgments his refusal might bring); the appeal to Pharaoh's interest (lest the judgment fall on Egypt too); the plea pressed. The verse displays Moses and Aaron pressing the request to sacrifice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the message — "The God of the Hebrews hath met with us: let us go... and sacrifice," the renewed appeal (Exodus 3, Ellicott, Gill); "three days' journey into the desert," the request (Exodus 8:26, Calvin, Henry); "lest he fall upon us with pestilence," the warning (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pressing Moses. The verse marks the message: God met with us. The verse displays the request: three days' journey. The verse displays the worship: to sacrifice. The verse displays the warning: of judgment. The request displays Moses pressing the demand. The verse displays the Moses: pleading. The verse displays the Pharaoh: pressed. The verse displays the worship: sought. Moses and Aaron pressing the request to sacrifice display the renewed appeal: the God of the Hebrews hath met with us; let us go to sacrifice (Exodus 3; 8:26) — the request to worship, with a warning of judgment, pressed on the defiant king. The verse displays the accusing Pharaoh (v.4).
4:
"And the king of Egypt said unto them, Wherefore do ye, Moses and Aaron, let the people from their works? get you unto your burdens." The very brief verse notes Pharaoh accusing Moses of idling the people. The grammatical voices note the accusation. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the king of Egypt said unto them, Wherefore do ye, Moses and Aaron, let the people from their works? get you unto your burdens" — Pharaoh accused Moses and Aaron of "letting" (hindering, distracting) the people from their labor, and bade them get back to their burdens; the contemptuous dismissal; the king regarding the request only as a slackening of the slaves' work; the refusal of the demand.

On "Wherefore do ye... let the people from their works?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the charge. Calvin and Clarke note Pharaoh's charge (that Moses and Aaron were distracting the slaves from their work) — the king's view (the worship-request a mere pretext for idleness, v.17); the contempt for the demand.

On "get you unto your burdens," the grammatical-historical note the dismissal: "get you unto your burdens" — Pharaoh ordering the people (or Moses and Aaron, reckoned among the slaves) back to their forced labor; the dismissal of the embassy; the refusal that would harden into the increased burden (vv.6-9). The verse displays Pharaoh accusing Moses of idling the people.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the accusation — "Wherefore do ye... let the people from their works? get you unto your burdens," the charge (Keil, Gill); "Wherefore do ye... let the people from their works?" the charge of idling (Calvin, Clarke); "get you unto your burdens," the dismissal (vv.6-9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the accusing king. The verse marks the charge: idling the people. The verse displays the dismissal: to the burdens. The verse displays the contempt: of the king. The verse displays the burden: to increase. The accusation displays Pharaoh dismissing the demand. The verse displays the Pharaoh: accusing. The verse displays the Moses: dismissed. The verse displays the burden: coming. Pharaoh accusing Moses of idling the people displays the contemptuous refusal: wherefore do ye let the people from their works? get you to your burdens (vv.6-9) — the king dismissing the demand as mere idleness, the prelude to the heavier burden. The verse displays the multiplied people (v.5).
5:
"And Pharaoh said, Behold, the people of the land now are many, and ye make them rest from their burdens." The very brief verse notes Pharaoh grudging the people any rest. The grammatical voices note the grudge. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Pharaoh said, Behold, the people of the land now are many, and ye make them rest from their burdens" — Pharaoh complained that the people (the Israelites, now many, Exodus 1:7) were being given rest from their labor by Moses and Aaron's intervention; the grudging of any respite; the king's view (the slaves should be kept ever at work, not given a holiday to worship); the hardness of the oppressor.

On "the people of the land now are many," the witnesses note the multitude. Gill notes "the people... are many" (the Israelites grown numerous, Exodus 1:7) — the very increase that Pharaoh feared (Exodus 1:9); the multitude he would keep enslaved.

On "ye make them rest from their burdens," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the grudge: "ye make them rest from their burdens" — Pharaoh grudging the slaves any rest (the request to worship seen as mere idleness); the hardness (the oppressor begrudging even a day's worship); the providence (the grudge that would harden into the heavier burden, vv.6-9). The verse displays Pharaoh grudging the people any rest.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the grudge — "the people of the land now are many, and ye make them rest from their burdens," the grudging (Exodus 1:7, Keil, Gill); "the people of the land now are many," the multitude (Exodus 1:9, Gill); "ye make them rest from their burdens," the grudge (vv.6-9, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the grudging king. The verse marks the people: many. The verse displays the rest: grudged. The verse displays the burden: to be kept. The verse displays the hardness: of the king. The grudge displays Pharaoh begrudging the slaves rest. The verse displays the Pharaoh: grudging. The verse displays the people: many. The verse displays the burden: to increase. Pharaoh grudging the people any rest displays the hardness of the oppressor: the people are many, and ye make them rest (Exodus 1:7, 9) — the king begrudging the slaves even a day's worship, the prelude to the heavier burden. The verse displays the cruel order (vv.6-9).
6:
"And Pharaoh commanded the same day the taskmasters of the people, and their officers, saying," The very brief verse notes Pharaoh's command to the taskmasters. The grammatical voices note the order. Ellicott and Gill note that "Pharaoh commanded the same day the taskmasters of the people, and their officers" — Pharaoh, the same day (in his anger at the demand, vv.1-5), issued a new command to the taskmasters (the Egyptian slave-drivers) and the officers (the Hebrew foremen, set over the people); the prompt cruelty; the order to increase the burden (vv.7-9); the retaliation for the request.

On "the taskmasters of the people, and their officers," the witnesses note the overseers. Gill notes the taskmasters (Egyptian) and officers (Hebrew foremen, v.14) — the chain of command over the forced labor; the instruments of the increased oppression.

On the command, the grammatical-historical note the cruelty: Pharaoh's command (the same day, in retaliation) — the prompt, vindictive cruelty (punishing the people for Moses' demand); the order to make the bondage harder (vv.7-9); the providence (the affliction worsening at the first attempt at deliverance, the trial of Israel's faith). The verse displays Pharaoh's command to the taskmasters.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the order — "Pharaoh commanded the same day the taskmasters... and their officers," the command (vv.1-5, Ellicott, Gill); "the taskmasters of the people, and their officers," the overseers (v.14, Gill); the cruelty (vv.7-9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanding king. The verse marks the command: the same day. The verse displays the taskmasters: charged. The verse displays the officers: the foremen. The verse displays the cruelty: prompt. The command displays Pharaoh's vindictive order. The verse displays the Pharaoh: commanding. The verse displays the taskmasters: charged. The verse displays the burden: to increase. Pharaoh's command to the taskmasters displays the prompt cruelty: the same day he commanded the taskmasters and officers (vv.7-9) — the vindictive order to harden the bondage in retaliation for the demand. The verse displays the order of no straw (v.7).
7:
"Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick, as heretofore: let them go and gather straw for themselves." The witnesses note the order to withhold straw from the brickmakers. On "Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick," the grammatical voices note the order. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick, as heretofore: let them go and gather straw for themselves" — Pharaoh ordered that the straw (chopped straw, mixed into the Nile-mud to bind the bricks) no longer be supplied to the Israelites; instead they must gather their own straw (stubble, v.12), yet make the same number of bricks (v.8); the cruel device (doubling the labor while keeping the quota); the increased bondage.

On "straw to make brick," the reformed and witnesses note the labor. Gill notes the straw (used in the Egyptian brick-making, mixed with the clay) — the material now withheld; the labor of gathering it added to the labor of brick-making.

On "let them go and gather straw for themselves," the grammatical-historical note the cruelty: "let them go and gather straw for themselves" — the people sent to gather their own straw (scattered through the fields, v.12); the cruel multiplying of the toil (the same quota, less means); the affliction worsened (the deliverance attempt bringing, at first, harder bondage); the providence (the trial that would make the deliverance the more glorious). The verse displays the order to withhold straw.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the order — "Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick... let them go and gather straw for themselves," the withholding (v.8, Keil, Gill); "straw to make brick," the material (Gill); "let them go and gather straw for themselves," the cruelty (v.12, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cruel order. The verse marks the straw: withheld. The verse displays the brick: still required. The verse displays the gathering: added. The verse displays the bondage: harder. The order displays the cruel multiplying of toil. The verse displays the Pharaoh: ordering. The verse displays the straw: withheld. The verse displays the affliction: worsened. The order to withhold straw displays the increased bondage: no more straw, but gather it yourselves (v.12) — the cruel device, the same quota with less means, the affliction worsened at the first attempt at deliverance. The verse displays the unchanged quota (v.8).
8:
"And the tale of the bricks, which they did make heretofore, ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof: for they be idle; therefore they cry, saying, Let us go and sacrifice to our God." The witnesses note the unchanged quota and Pharaoh's charge of idleness. On "the tale of the bricks... ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof," the grammatical voices note the quota. Gill and Clarke note that "the tale (number, count) of the bricks, which they did make heretofore, ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof" — the full daily quota of bricks was still required, though the straw was withheld (v.7); the cruel impossibility (the same number with double the labor); the design to crush the people.

On "ye shall not diminish ought thereof," the witnesses note the cruelty. Gill notes the quota unchanged (no reduction allowed) — the breaking burden; the cruelty of the unattainable demand.

On "for they be idle; therefore they cry, saying, Let us go and sacrifice," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the slander: "for they be idle; therefore they cry, Let us go and sacrifice to our God" — Pharaoh's charge that the people were idle (and so had leisure to think of worship); the slander (the worship-request branded as the pretext of idle slaves); the contempt for the service of God (the worldly view that counts worship idleness); the hardening of the oppressor (turning the holy desire into an accusation). The verse displays the unchanged quota and the charge of idleness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the quota — "the tale of the bricks... ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof," the unchanged demand (Gill, Clarke); "ye shall not diminish ought thereof," the cruelty (Gill); "for they be idle; therefore they cry, Let us go and sacrifice," the slander (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cruel quota. The verse marks the tale: unchanged. The verse displays the burden: impossible. The verse displays the slander: they be idle. The verse displays the worship: branded idleness. The quota displays the breaking, impossible burden. The verse displays the Pharaoh: demanding. The verse displays the people: crushed. The verse displays the worship: slandered. The unchanged quota and the charge of idleness display the crushing burden and the slander: the full tale of bricks, for they be idle and cry to sacrifice (v.17) — the impossible demand, the holy desire branded as the pretext of idle slaves. The verse displays the harder bondage (v.9).
9:
"Let there more work be laid upon the men, that they may labour therein; and let them not regard vain words." The witnesses note Pharaoh laying heavier work to silence the demand — the crux of the worsened affliction. On "Let there more work be laid upon the men, that they may labour therein," the grammatical voices note the burden. Ellicott and Gill note that "Let there more work be laid upon the men, that they may labour therein" — Pharaoh ordered heavier work laid on the Israelites, that they might be kept toiling; the design (to crush their spirit and leave them no leisure to listen to Moses); the increased bondage; the cruelty meant to break the people.

On the heavier burden, interpreters note the design. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Pharaoh's purpose — the heavier work designed to occupy and crush the people, so that they would have no time or heart to think of deliverance; the cruel policy (the more the deliverance was sought, the heavier the bondage grew); the providence — the strange and trying way of God, that the first effect of Moses' coming was not relief but harder bondage; the trial of Israel's faith (the deliverance seeming, at first, to make things worse, that the people might learn to trust God and not the means, and that the final deliverance might be the more glorious, by God's hand alone, against the hardened oppressor); the lesson — God's deliverances often begin with deeper trouble (the darkest hour before the dawn).

On "let them not regard vain words," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the contempt: "let them not regard vain words" — Pharaoh branding Moses' message (the word of God, "Let my people go," v.1) as "vain (or false) words"; the contempt for God's word (the proud king counting the divine promise empty talk, as Sennacherib, 2 Kings 18:20); the design to keep the people from heeding the promise of deliverance; the hardening that despised God's word. The verse displays Pharaoh laying heavier work to silence the demand.

The witnesses agree the substance: Pharaoh laid heavier work on the people and branded God's word "vain words" (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the worsened affliction: "let there more work be laid... let them not regard vain words" — the heavier bondage designed to crush the people's spirit and silence the demand; the strange providence that the first effect of the deliverer's coming was harder bondage (the trial of faith, the deliverance seeming to make things worse, that the people might trust God alone and the final deliverance be the more glorious); Pharaoh's contempt of God's word as "vain words" (2 Kings 18:20). The verse displays the worsened-affliction crux. The verse marks the burden: heavier. The verse displays the design: to crush. The verse displays the word: branded vain. The verse displays the trial: of faith. The burden displays Pharaoh crushing the people to silence God's word. The verse displays the Pharaoh: hardening. The verse displays the people: crushed. The verse displays the word: despised. Pharaoh laying heavier work to silence the demand displays the crux: more work, and let them not regard vain words (2 Kings 18:20) — the harder bondage at the first attempt at deliverance, the trial of faith, Pharaoh's contempt of God's word as empty talk. The verse displays the scattered people (vv.10-13).
10:
"And the taskmasters of the people went out, and their officers, and they spake to the people, saying, Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw." The very brief verse notes the taskmasters publishing Pharaoh's cruel order. The grammatical voices note the order. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "the taskmasters of the people went out, and their officers, and they spake to the people, saying, Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw" — the taskmasters and officers went out and announced Pharaoh's order to the people: no more straw would be given; the cruel decree published; the burden laid (vv.7-9 carried out); the people to gather their own straw yet make the full quota.

On "Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the contrast. Henry and Gill note "Thus saith Pharaoh" — the proud king's decree (a dark echo of "Thus saith the LORD," v.1; the word of the oppressor set against the word of God); the announcement of the cruelty; the burden imposed.

On the publishing, the grammatical-historical note the cruelty: the taskmasters publishing the order — the cruel decree carried out (no straw, full quota); the affliction worsened; the providence (the trial of Israel under the hardened bondage). The verse displays the taskmasters publishing Pharaoh's cruel order.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the order — "Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw," the decree (vv.7-9, Keil, Henry); "Thus saith Pharaoh," the proud echo (v.1, Henry, Gill); the cruelty (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the publishing taskmasters. The verse marks the order: no straw. The verse displays the Pharaoh: thus saith. The verse displays the burden: imposed. The verse displays the affliction: worsened. The publishing displays the cruel decree carried out. The verse displays the taskmasters: announcing. The verse displays the people: burdened. The verse displays the bondage: harder. The taskmasters publishing Pharaoh's cruel order display the decree carried out: thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw (vv.7-9; v.1) — the word of the oppressor set against the word of God, the affliction worsened. The verse displays the scattered people (vv.11-12).
11:
"Go ye, get you straw where ye can find it: yet not ought of your work shall be diminished." The very brief verse notes the people sent for straw with no reduction of work. The grammatical voices note the command. Gill notes that "Go ye, get you straw where ye can find it: yet not ought of your work shall be diminished" — the people were sent to find straw wherever they could, yet without any reduction of their work (the full quota of bricks still required); the cruel impossibility; the burden doubled (the gathering of straw added, the quota unchanged); the affliction at its height.

On "Go ye, get you straw where ye can find it," the witnesses note the toil. Gill notes the people sent to scour the land for straw (stubble, v.12) — the added, scattered labor; the burden multiplied.

On "yet not ought of your work shall be diminished," the grammatical-historical note the cruelty: "yet not ought of your work shall be diminished" — the quota unchanged (no allowance for the added labor); the breaking, impossible demand; the affliction worsened; the providence (the trial of the people under the cruel bondage). The verse displays the people sent for straw with no reduction of work.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Go ye, get you straw... yet not ought of your work shall be diminished," the cruel demand (Gill); "Go ye, get you straw where ye can find it," the toil (v.12, Gill); "yet not ought of your work shall be diminished," the cruelty (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the burdened people. The verse marks the straw: to be found. The verse displays the work: undiminished. The verse displays the burden: doubled. The verse displays the cruelty: impossible. The command displays the breaking burden. The verse displays the people: sent. The verse displays the work: full. The verse displays the affliction: worsened. The people sent for straw with no reduction of work display the impossible burden: get straw, yet your work shall not be diminished (v.12) — the gathering added, the quota unchanged, the affliction at its height. The verse displays the scattered people (v.12).
12:
"So the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble instead of straw." The very brief verse notes the people scattered to gather stubble. The grammatical voices note the scattering. Ellicott and Gill note that "the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble instead of straw" — the Israelites were scattered through all Egypt, gathering stubble (the short, dry stalks left after harvest) in place of the straw now withheld; the wearisome, scattered labor; the burden multiplied (the search for stubble added to the brick-making); the affliction.

On "scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt," the witnesses note the toil. Gill notes the people scattered (searching far and wide for stubble) — the laborious gathering; the burden of the search.

On "to gather stubble instead of straw," the grammatical-historical note the makeshift: "to gather stubble instead of straw" — the stubble (an inferior substitute for the proper straw) gathered with great toil; the cruel multiplying of the labor; the affliction worsened; the providence (the trial of the people, the bondage at its height before the deliverance). The verse displays the people scattered to gather stubble.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the scattering — "the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble," the toil (Ellicott, Gill); "scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt," the labor (Gill); "to gather stubble instead of straw," the makeshift (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the scattered people. The verse marks the scattering: through Egypt. The verse displays the stubble: gathered. The verse displays the labor: wearisome. The verse displays the affliction: worsened. The scattering displays the multiplied toil of the people. The verse displays the people: scattered. The verse displays the stubble: instead of straw. The verse displays the burden: heavier. The people scattered to gather stubble display the multiplied toil: scattered through Egypt to gather stubble instead of straw — the wearisome search added to the brick-making, the affliction at its height. The verse displays the driven taskmasters (v.13).
13:
"And the taskmasters hasted them, saying, Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw." The very brief verse notes the taskmasters driving the people to the full quota. The grammatical voices note the driving. Keil and Delitzsch and JFB note that "the taskmasters hasted them, saying, Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw" — the taskmasters drove (hurried, pressed) the people to fulfill the full daily quota of bricks, just as when straw was supplied; the relentless driving; the impossible demand (the same tasks, no straw); the cruelty pressing the exhausted people.

On "the taskmasters hasted them," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the pressure. JFB and Ellicott note the taskmasters' driving (the relentless pressure, the slave-drivers urging the people on) — the harsh cruelty; the impossible demand pressed.

On "Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw," the grammatical-historical note the impossibility: "Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw" — the full quota demanded (as if straw were still given); the cruel, impossible standard; the affliction (the people unable to meet the quota, vv.14); the providence (the bondage at its bitterest). The verse displays the taskmasters driving the people.

14:
"And the officers of the children of Israel, which Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, and demanded, Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your task in making brick both yesterday and to day, as heretofore?" The witnesses note the Hebrew officers beaten for the unmet quota. On "the officers of the children of Israel... were beaten," the grammatical voices note the beating. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the officers of the children of Israel, which Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten" — the Hebrew officers (foremen, set over the people by the Egyptian taskmasters) were beaten because the people, lacking straw, could not make the full quota; the cruelty falling on the foremen; the impossible demand punished with stripes; the suffering of the leaders for the people's unmet quota.

On "were beaten," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the cruelty. Wesley and Gill note the beating (the Hebrew foremen scourged for the shortfall) — the cruelty of the impossible demand; the innocent foremen suffering for what they could not prevent; the affliction.

On "Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your task... both yesterday and to day," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the impossibility: "Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your task in making brick both yesterday and to day, as heretofore?" — the demand (why the quota was not met, as before); the impossible standard (the same quota, no straw); the unjust punishment; the affliction (the bondage at its bitterest, driving the officers to appeal to Pharaoh, vv.15-16). The verse displays the Hebrew officers beaten for the unmet quota.

The thin comment matches the verse. 15:
"Then the officers of the children of Israel came and cried unto Pharaoh, saying, Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?" The witnesses note the officers appealing to Pharaoh. On "the officers of the children of Israel came and cried unto Pharaoh," the grammatical voices note the appeal. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "the officers of the children of Israel came and cried unto Pharaoh, saying, Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?" — the Hebrew foremen, beaten (v.14), came and appealed to Pharaoh himself, crying out at the injustice of the impossible demand; the appeal to the king (not knowing the order was Pharaoh's own); the cry of the oppressed; the protest at the cruelty.

On "came and cried unto Pharaoh," the reformed and witnesses note the appeal. Ellicott and Gill note the officers' appeal (to the king, seeking relief) — the cry against the injustice; the appeal to the very source of the cruelty (unknowing); the protest of the wronged.

On "Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?" the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the protest: "Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?" — the officers' protest at the unjust dealing (the impossible demand, the beating); the appeal to Pharaoh's justice (in vain, the order being his own, v.17); the cry of the oppressed to the oppressor. The verse displays the officers appealing to Pharaoh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appeal — "the officers... came and cried unto Pharaoh... 16:
"There is no straw given unto thy servants, and they say to us, Make brick: and, behold, thy servants are beaten; but the fault is in thine own people." The very brief verse notes the officers laying the fault on Pharaoh's people. The grammatical voices note the protest. On "There is no straw given... and they say to us, Make brick," the witnesses note the injustice. Gill notes the impossible demand (no straw, yet brick required) — the injustice protested; the cruelty of the Egyptian taskmasters.

The verse displays the officers laying the fault on Pharaoh's people.

17:
"But he said, Ye are idle, ye are idle: therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD." The witnesses note Pharaoh branding the people idle. On "Ye are idle, ye are idle," the grammatical voices note the charge. Ellicott and Gill note that "he said, Ye are idle, ye are idle: therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD" — Pharaoh, rejecting the officers' appeal, twice charged them with idleness ("ye are idle, ye are idle") — the contemptuous accusation (the people had too much leisure, hence their desire to go and worship); the dismissal of the appeal; the hardening of the king.

On "ye are idle," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the slander. Calvin and Clarke note the doubled charge of idleness — the slander (the worldly view that counts the worship of God idleness, the desire to serve God a mark of having too little to do); the contempt for the service of God; the hardening of the oppressor.

On "therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD," the grammatical-historical note the contempt: "therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD" — Pharaoh branding the holy desire (to worship God, v.1) as the pretext of idleness; the contempt of God's service (the worldly heart that sees devotion as laziness); the hardening that despised both God and his worship; the refusal that pressed the bondage harder (v.18). The verse displays Pharaoh branding the people idle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "Ye are idle, ye are idle: therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice," the slander (Ellicott, Gill); "Ye are idle, ye are idle," the contempt (Calvin, Clarke); "therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD," the contempt of worship (grammatical-historical). 18:
"Go therefore now, and work; for there shall no straw be given you, yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks." The very brief verse notes Pharaoh's final refusal and the impossible demand. The grammatical voices note the refusal. Gill notes that "Go therefore now, and work; for there shall no straw be given you, yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks" — Pharaoh dismissed the officers with the impossible demand: no straw, yet the full quota of bricks; the flat refusal of relief; the hardening of the king; the bondage at its bitterest (the same quota, no means).

On "for there shall no straw be given you," the witnesses note the cruelty. Gill notes the refusal of straw (the means withheld) — the cruelty unrelenting; the impossible demand confirmed.

On "yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks," the grammatical-historical note the impossibility: "yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks" — the full quota still required (no straw, full number); the breaking, impossible demand; the affliction (the people crushed, the officers in despair, vv.19-21); the providence (the bondage at its height before the deliverance). The verse displays Pharaoh's final refusal and the impossible demand.

19:
"And the officers of the children of Israel did see that they were in evil case, after it was said, Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task." The very brief verse notes the officers seeing their desperate plight. The grammatical voices note the despair. JFB and Gill note that "the officers of the children of Israel did see that they were in evil case, after it was said, Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task" — the Hebrew foremen saw they were in a desperate plight (evil case): the quota would not be reduced, the straw not given, and they themselves beaten; the despair (no relief, the impossible demand fixed); the bitterness of the bondage; the plight that turned their distress against Moses (vv.20-21).

On "did see that they were in evil case," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the despair. Calvin and Clarke note the officers' despair (seeing no way out, the demand impossible, the beating unjust) — the misery of the bondage; the plight that bred their reproach of Moses (v.21).

On "Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task," the grammatical-historical note the fixity: "Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task" — the quota fixed (no reduction); the impossible demand confirmed; the despair (the officers in evil case, turning to blame Moses and Aaron, vv.20-21); the providence (the trial at its bitterest). The verse displays the officers seeing their desperate plight.

20:

On "Moses and Aaron, who stood in the way," the witnesses note the waiting. Gill notes Moses and Aaron standing in the way (waiting to hear the result of the appeal) — the deliverers awaiting the outcome; the meeting with the despairing officers.

On "as they came forth from Pharaoh," the grammatical-historical note the occasion: "as they came forth from Pharaoh" — the officers coming from the rejected appeal (vv.17-18); the meeting that occasioned the reproach (v.21); the providence (the distress that would drive Moses to God, vv.22-23). The verse displays the officers meeting Moses and Aaron.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 21:
"And they said unto them, The LORD look upon you, and judge; because ye have made our savour to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of his servants, to put a sword in their hand to slay us." The witnesses note the officers reproaching Moses for their worsened plight. On "The LORD look upon you, and judge," the grammatical voices note the reproach. Ellicott and Gill note that "they said unto them, The LORD look upon you, and judge; because ye have made our savour to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh" — the officers, in their distress, reproached Moses and Aaron, calling on the LORD to judge them; the bitter complaint (blaming the deliverers for the worsened bondage); the reproach of the despairing (turning on the very men sent to deliver them).

On "ye have made our savour to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the charge. Wesley and Clarke note the charge — Moses and Aaron had made Israel's "savour" (their name, repute) abhorred (a stench) before Pharaoh; the blame (that the demand had only angered the king and worsened the bondage); the complaint of the oppressed (the deliverance attempt seeming to make things worse, the people's faith failing under the trial).

On "to put a sword in their hand to slay us," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the despair: "to put a sword in their hand to slay us" — the officers fearing the bondage would now destroy them (the sword in Pharaoh's hand); the despair (blaming Moses for their danger); the weakness of faith under the trial (the people, lately believing, Exodus 4:31, now turning against the deliverers when the affliction worsened); the providence (the trial that would drive Moses to God, vv.22-23, and prepare the people for the deliverance by God's hand alone). The verse displays the officers reproaching Moses.

The thin comment matches the verse. 22:
"And Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, Lord, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?" The witnesses note Moses' complaint to God at the worsened affliction — the crux of faith perplexed. On "Moses returned unto the LORD," the grammatical voices note the recourse. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, Lord, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people?" — Moses, distressed by the people's reproach (v.21) and the worsened bondage, returned to the LORD (in prayer); the recourse to God (the right resort of the troubled servant, carrying his perplexity to God); the laying of the burden before the LORD.

On "wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people?" interpreters expound the complaint. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Moses' words — "wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?": Moses complained (expostulated) to God: why had God dealt so ill with the people (the affliction worsening since Moses came), and why had God sent him (the mission seeming to fail)? — the perplexity of faith (Moses not understanding God's strange way, the deliverance beginning with deeper trouble); the witnesses balance: the complaint shows the weakness and impatience of Moses' faith (a fault, the murmuring of perplexity, questioning God's dealings); yet it was the complaint of faith, not unbelief — Moses brought his trouble to God (not casting off the mission, but seeking God in his perplexity); the honest wrestling of the servant who did not yet see God's design; the lesson — faith may be perplexed and complain, yet still hold to God and bring its trouble to him (as the Psalms of lament, Psalm 13:1-2; Habakkuk 1:2-3).

On "why is it that thou hast sent me?" the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the trial: "why is it that thou hast sent me?" — Moses questioning his mission (the deliverance seeming to fail, the bondage worse); the trial of the deliverer's faith (the strange providence, that God's sending had, at first, worsened things); the perplexity that God would answer with the renewed promise (Exodus 6:1-8, "now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh"); the faith that, though perplexed, returned to God. The verse displays Moses' complaint to God at the worsened affliction.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses, distressed, returned to God and complained that the affliction had worsened and his mission seemed to fail (Keil, Calvin, Henry, Wesley). The verse bears its weight as the crux of faith perplexed: "wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why hast thou sent me?" — the perplexity of Moses' faith at God's strange way (the deliverance beginning with deeper trouble); a complaint showing the weakness and impatience of faith, yet the complaint of faith, not unbelief — Moses bringing his trouble to God (as the laments, Psalm 13:1-2; Habakkuk 1:2-3), the honest wrestling of the servant who did not yet see God's design, soon answered with the renewed promise (Exodus 6:1-8). The verse displays the faith-perplexed crux. The verse marks the recourse: returned to God. The verse displays the complaint: why evil entreated. The verse displays the perplexity: of faith. The verse displays the trust: still in God. The complaint displays Moses' perplexed faith carried to God. The verse displays the Moses: complaining. The verse displays the God: appealed to. The verse displays the trial: of faith. Moses' complaint to God at the worsened affliction displays the crux: wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why hast thou sent me? (Psalm 13:1-2; Habakkuk 1:2-3) — the perplexity of faith at God's strange way, the honest wrestling of the servant, brought to God, soon answered (Exodus 6:1-8). The verse displays the answered Moses (v.23; ch.6).
23:
"For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people; neither hast thou delivered thy people at all." The witnesses note Moses' lament that no deliverance had come. On "since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people," the grammatical voices note the worsening. Gill and Clarke note that "since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people" — Moses lamented that since he came to Pharaoh in God's name (v.1), Pharaoh had only done worse evil to the people (the harder bondage, vv.6-18); the deliverance attempt seeming to fail; the perplexity (the mission bringing, at first, more affliction).

On "he hath done evil to this people," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the trial. Calvin and Gill note Moses' lament (the bondage worse since his coming) — the trial of faith (the strange providence); the perplexity of the servant (not seeing God's design).

On "neither hast thou delivered thy people at all," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the lament: "neither hast thou delivered thy people at all" — Moses' complaint that God had not delivered at all (no sign of deliverance, only worse bondage); the impatience of faith (expecting a quicker deliverance); yet the complaint brought to God (the honest lament, soon answered, Exodus 6:1, "now shalt thou see what I will do"); the providence (the deliverance only delayed, not denied, the trial preparing the glorious deliverance by God's hand). The verse displays Moses' lament that no deliverance had come.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the worsening — "since I came to Pharaoh... he hath done evil to this people," the harder bondage (vv.6-18, Gill, Clarke); "he hath done evil to this people," the trial (Calvin, Gill); "neither hast thou delivered thy people at all," the lament (Exodus 6:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the lamenting Moses. The verse marks the coming: in thy name. The verse displays the evil: done to the people. The verse displays the deliverance: not yet. The verse displays the lament: of faith. The lament displays Moses' perplexity at the delay. The verse displays the Moses: lamenting. The verse displays the deliverance: awaited. The verse displays the trial: of faith. Moses' lament that no deliverance had come displays the perplexity of faith: he hath done evil, neither hast thou delivered thy people at all (vv.6-18; Exodus 6:1) — the deliverance seeming to fail, the honest lament brought to God, soon answered. The verse closes the chapter, before God's renewed promise (ch.6).
